parts.homeLangDetails: The root consonant k (ك) is part of the trilateral root ك-ت-ب (k-t-b), the core meaning of which is 'writing.' Arabic roots are built from consonants, and the vowels woven around them signal grammatical function — tense, role, derivation.
The short vowel pattern fatḥa-fatḥa-a (كَتَبَ) marks the past tense, third person masculine singular: 'he wrote.'
The second root consonant t (ت) of the trilateral root ك-ت-ب.
The third root consonant b (ب) of the trilateral root ك-ت-ب.

words.exampleTranslation: The student wrote a long letter.
words.flag: 5+ parts — Arabic trilateral root ك-ت-ب split into three consonants plus vowel pattern

parts.homeLangDetails: Built on the root ك-ت-ب (k-t-b, 'writing') using the فاعِل (fāʿil) pattern, which in Arabic is the standard active participle pattern meaning 'one who does X.' It works exactly like the English -er suffix: كاتِب literally means 'one who writes,' i.e., a writer or scribe.

words.exampleTranslation: He is a famous writer in his country.
words.flag: only 1 part — the fāʿil pattern and root are fused into a single undecomposable word form

parts.homeLangDetails: The مَفعَل (mafʿal) pattern applied to the root ك-ت-ب creates a noun of place or instrument — here, 'a place for writing.' On its own, مَكتَب means 'desk' or 'office.'

The suffix ة (tāʾ marbūṭa) here extends the place-noun into a larger or more general institution. مَكتَبة means a library or bookshop — a whole establishment built around books and writing, rather than a single desk or office.

words.exampleTranslation: I spend long hours in the library.

parts.homeLangDetails: The pattern فِعال (fiʿāl) applied to the root ك-ت-ب gives a noun referring to the result or product of the root action. كِتاب is the thing that was written — a book or document. This same pattern yields words like جِواب (answer) and حِساب (account/calculation) across Arabic.

words.exampleTranslation: I read this book twice.
words.flag: only 1 part — root and word pattern are fully fused

parts.homeLangDetails: The pattern مَفعول (mafʿūl) is the standard passive participle in Arabic, equivalent to English past participles used as adjectives or nouns: 'written.' Applied to ك-ت-ب, مَكتوب means 'something written' — a letter, a message, or (in a fatalistic sense) 'what is destined,' since fate is seen as written by God.

words.translationLong: written, a letter, a message, (what is) destined
words.exampleTranslation: I received a letter from my old friend.
words.flag: only 1 part — passive participle pattern and root are fully fused

parts.homeLangDetails: The base noun from the root ك-ت-ب using the فِعال pattern — 'the written thing' or 'writing' as a product. Here it serves as the stem for the verbal noun of activity.

When added to certain noun stems, ة (tāʾ marbūṭa) shifts the meaning toward an abstract activity or process. كِتابة means 'the act of writing' — writing as a practice or craft, comparable to English '-ing' or '-tion' in 'writing' or 'inscription.'

words.translationLong: writing, the act of writing, penmanship
words.exampleTranslation: Writing is a skill that requires practice.

parts.homeLangDetails: The first consonant of the root د-ر-س (d-r-s), whose core meaning is 'to study, to learn, to investigate.'
The vowel pattern fatḥa-fatḥa-a marks the past tense, third person masculine singular: 'he studied.'
The second consonant of the root د-ر-س.
The third consonant of the root د-ر-س.

words.translationLong: he studied, he learned, he researched
words.exampleSentence: دَرَسَ الطالبُ اللغةَ العربيةَ لثلاث سنوات.
words.exampleTranslation: The student studied Arabic for three years.
words.flag: 5+ parts — Arabic trilateral root د-ر-س split into three consonants plus vowel pattern

parts.homeLangDetails: The فِعال (fiʿāl) pattern applied to the root د-ر-س (d-r-s, 'study'). This pattern typically forms verbal nouns — nouns describing an action or process. Here it gives the sense of 'studying' or 'study' as an activity.
Adds a final tāʾ marbūṭa to mark the verbal noun as a feminine abstract noun. دِراسة means 'the act of studying' or 'a course of study,' much like 'study' or 'studies' in English.

words.translationLong: study, studying, a course of study, academic research
words.exampleSentence: انتهت دِراستُها في الجامعة العام الماضي.
words.exampleTranslation: Her studies at the university ended last year.

parts.homeLangDetails: The فاعِل (fāʿil) active participle pattern applied to the root د-ر-س (d-r-s, 'study'). Just like كاتِب (writer) from كتب, دارِس means 'one who studies' — a student, scholar, or researcher. The pattern is consistently the Arabic equivalent of the English -er suffix for agents.

words.exampleTranslation: He is a student of Islamic history.
words.flag: only 1 part — fāʿil pattern and root are fully fused

parts.homeLangDetails: The مَفعَل (mafʿal) pattern applied to the root د-ر-س (d-r-s, 'study') creates a noun of place — 'a place where studying happens.' This is the same pattern as مَكتَب (desk/office) from ك-ت-ب.

The tāʾ marbūṭa marks this as a feminine noun and signals an established institution. مَدرَسة is a school — a place formally dedicated to study. The English word 'madrasa' is a direct borrowing of this word.

words.exampleSentence: تذهب بناتي إلى المَدرَسة كلَّ صباح.
words.exampleTranslation: My daughters go to school every morning.

parts.homeLangDetails: Built on the root د-ر-س (d-r-s, 'study') using the مُفَعِّل (mufaʿʿil) pattern, which is the active participle of Form II Arabic verbs. Form II doubles the middle root consonant and typically adds an intensive or causative meaning — here, 'one who causes others to study,' i.e., a teacher or instructor. Compare دارِس (one who studies) vs. مُدَرِّس (one who makes others study).

words.exampleTranslation: The teacher explains the lesson clearly.
words.flag: only 1 part — causative Form II pattern and root are fully fused

parts.homeLangDetails: The broken plural of دَرس (a lesson, a session of study), itself built from the root د-ر-س. Arabic 'broken plurals' change the internal vowel pattern of the word rather than adding a suffix — like English 'foot/feet' or 'tooth/teeth,' but far more systematically. The pattern فُعول (fuʿūl) is a common broken plural form for simple nouns.

words.exampleTranslation: I reviewed my lessons before the exam.
words.flag: only 1 part — broken plural pattern and root are fully fused

parts.homeLangDetails: Built on the root ع-ل-م (ʿ-l-m), whose core meaning relates to 'knowledge' and 'knowing.' The doubled middle consonant (لّ) marks this as a Form II verb, which in Arabic typically gives a causative or intensive sense. So while عَلِمَ means 'to know,' عَلَّمَ means 'to cause to know' — that is, to teach. The past tense is marked by the fatḥa vowel pattern and the final -a.

words.translationLong: he taught, he instructed, he trained
words.exampleTranslation: The teacher taught his students reading and writing.
words.flag: only 1 part — Form II causative pattern and root are fully fused

parts.homeLangDetails: A core noun from the root ع-ل-م (ʿ-l-m, 'to know'). The فِعل (fiʿl) pattern gives a noun of essence — the thing itself, not the act. عِلم means knowledge or science as a body of understanding. It is related to عَلِمَ (to know), عالِم (one who knows), and is the source of the -ilm in words like 'alchemy' (via Arabic الكيمياء) and cognates across many fields.

words.translationLong: knowledge, science, learning, a branch of study
words.exampleTranslation: Knowledge is light and ignorance is darkness.
words.flag: only 1 part — root and word pattern are fully fused

parts.homeLangDetails: The فاعِل (fāʿil) active participle of the root ع-ل-م (ʿ-l-m, 'to know'). Just as كاتِب means 'one who writes' and دارِس means 'one who studies,' عالِم means 'one who knows' — a scholar, scientist, or learned person. Interestingly, the word عالَم (world, universe) shares the same root: the world is 'that which is known' or 'the realm of knowledge.'

words.translationLong: scholar, scientist, learned person, one who knows
words.exampleSentence: كان ابنُ سينا عالِماً عظيماً في الطب والفلسفة.
words.exampleTranslation: Ibn Sina was a great scholar in medicine and philosophy.
words.flag: only 1 part — fāʿil pattern and root are fully fused

parts.homeLangDetails: The prefix مُتَ marks the active participle of Form V verbs (the تَفَعَّلَ pattern), indicating 'one who is doing or has done' the action. It often implies a reflexive or intensive sense.

From the root ع-ل-م (knowledge). In Form V (تَعَلَّمَ), this root shifts meaning from 'to teach' (Form II: عَلَّمَ) to 'to learn' — the reflexive receiving of teaching. The doubled lam (ّ) is characteristic of this form.
The final م closes the Form V active participle pattern (مُتَفَعِّل), marking the word as a noun/adjective meaning 'one who learns.'

words.translationLong: learner, student, one who is learning
words.exampleTranslation: He is a diligent learner in the class.

parts.homeLangDetails: The prefix مَ, combined with the vowel pattern, forms the passive participle in Arabic (مَفعول pattern). It marks something as 'that which has been acted upon' — here, 'that which has been made known.'
From the root ع-ل-م, meaning knowledge. In the مَفعول passive participle pattern, this gives مَعلوم — 'that which is known.'
The feminine ending تاء مربوطة (ة) turns the adjective/participle مَعلوم ('known') into a concrete singular noun: 'a piece of information,' one item of what is known.

words.translationLong: a piece of information, a fact
words.exampleTranslation: This is a very important piece of information.

parts.homeLangDetails: The prefix مَ, combined with the vowel pattern, forms the passive participle in Arabic (مَفعول pattern). It marks something as 'that which has been made known.'
From the root ع-ل-م, meaning knowledge. In the مَفعول passive participle pattern, this gives مَعلوم — 'that which is known.'
The suffix ات is the standard feminine sound plural in Arabic, attached here to مَعلومة to form its plural: 'pieces of information,' or collectively, 'information.'

words.exampleTranslation: I need more information.

parts.homeLangDetails: A bare Form I triliteral verb root ف-ه-م (f-h-m), meaning 'to understand.' The vowel pattern فَعِلَ (fatha-kasra-fatha) is the past tense conjugation for the third-person masculine singular. There are no separable prefixes or suffixes here — the root and the grammatical pattern are fused.

words.translationLong: to understand (past tense, 3rd person masculine singular)
words.exampleTranslation: The student understood the lesson well.

parts.homeLangDetails: A verbal noun (مصدر, maṣdar) from the root ف-ه-م. The pattern فَعْل is one of the most common maṣdar patterns in Arabic for Form I verbs, converting the action 'to understand' into the abstract noun 'understanding' or 'comprehension.'

words.exampleTranslation: Understanding is more important than memorization.

parts.homeLangDetails: The prefix مَ, in the مَفعول pattern, creates the passive participle: 'that which has been understood.' This form very commonly becomes a standalone noun in Arabic, describing the result or object of a verb.

From the root ف-ه-م (to understand). The مَفعول vowel pattern applied to this root gives مَفهوم — literally 'that which is understood,' but now fully established as a noun meaning 'concept' or 'notion.'

words.exampleTranslation: This is an ancient philosophical concept.

parts.homeLangDetails: The active participle of the Form I verb فَهِمَ (to understand), following the فاعِل pattern — the most productive active participle pattern in Arabic. It means 'one who understands' or, as an adjective, 'understanding.' The long vowel ا after the first root letter is the hallmark of this pattern.

words.exampleTranslation: Do you understand what I said?

parts.homeLangDetails: A bare Form I triliteral verb from the root ق-ر-أ (q-r-ʾ), meaning 'to read' or 'to recite.' The vowel pattern فَعَلَ (three fathas) marks the past tense, third-person masculine singular. The root and grammatical pattern are fused — there are no separable affixes.

words.translationLong: to read (past tense, 3rd person masculine singular)
words.exampleTranslation: The boy read the whole story.

parts.homeLangDetails: A verbal noun (مصدر) from the root ق-ر-أ (to read/recite). The pattern قِعَالة (with a long vowel ا in the middle) is common for maṣdars of Form I verbs whose action has a sense of craft, process, or repeated activity.
The تاء مربوطة (ة) here is part of the maṣdar pattern rather than a grammatical addition — it finalizes the abstract noun form 'reading/recitation' as a feminine noun.

words.exampleTranslation: Reading is nourishment for the mind.

parts.homeLangDetails: The active participle of قَرَأَ (to read), following the فاعِل pattern. It means 'one who reads' or 'a reader.' The long ا after the first root letter and the kasra on the third are characteristic of this pattern. When the root ends in a hamza (أ), it appears at the end as ئ.

words.exampleTranslation: He is an avid reader of books.

parts.homeLangDetails: The prefix مَ in the مَفعول pattern forms the passive participle: 'that which has been read.' From the root ق-ر-أ, this gives مَقروء — 'read' (as in, something that has been read).

From the root ق-ر-أ (to read). In the مَفعول pattern the hamza (أ) shifts to و, giving مَقروء — 'that which has been read.' It functions as an adjective ('readable,' 'legible') or as a noun referring to something read.

words.translationLong: that which has been read; readable, legible
words.exampleSentence: هذا النص غير مَقروء بسبب الخط الرديء.
words.exampleTranslation: This text is illegible because of the poor handwriting.

parts.homeLangDetails: A bare Form I triliteral verb from the root ع-م-ل (ʿ-m-l), meaning 'to work' or 'to do.' The vowel pattern فَعِلَ (fatha-kasra-fatha) marks the past tense, third-person masculine singular. Root and pattern are fully fused — no separable affixes.

words.translationLong: to work, to do (past tense, 3rd person masculine singular)
words.exampleTranslation: The man worked all day long.

parts.homeLangDetails: A verbal noun (مصدر) from the root ع-م-ل (to work, to do). The pattern فَعَل is a very common maṣdar pattern in Arabic, turning the verb into an abstract noun meaning 'work,' 'labor,' or 'deed.' It is also the word used for 'job' or 'employment' in modern Arabic.

words.exampleTranslation: Do you have a suitable job?

parts.homeLangDetails: The active participle of عَمِلَ (to work), following the فاعِل pattern. It means 'one who works' or 'a worker.' The long ا after the first root letter and the kasra on the third root letter are characteristic of this pattern. It can also mean 'factor' in modern usage (e.g., عامِل مشترك, a common factor).

words.exampleTranslation: The worker deserves his wage.

parts.homeLangDetails: The prefix مَ in the مَفعَل/مَفعِل pattern creates a noun of place or instrument — 'the place where something is done.' From ع-م-ل (to work), مَعمَل is literally 'the place of work,' i.e., a laboratory or workshop.
From the root ع-م-ل (to work, to do). Combined with the مَفعَل place-noun pattern, this gives مَعمَل — 'workplace,' and by specialization, 'laboratory' in modern Arabic.

words.exampleTranslation: The students conducted their experiment in the laboratory.

parts.homeLangDetails: Plural of عامِل (worker), itself from the root ع-م-ل meaning 'to work/act'. This is not a transparent morpheme split but a root-and-pattern word: the plural pattern فُعّال applied to the root gives عُمّال.

words.exampleTranslation: The workers work hard every day.

parts.homeLangDetails: The prefix اِستِ- (from the pattern اِستِفعال) is a very productive Arabic prefix indicating 'seeking out' or 'putting to use'. Here it transforms the root ع-م-ل ('work/act') into the idea of 'putting something to work' — i.e., using it.
From the root ع-م-ل, the fundamental idea of working, doing, or acting. The same root gives عامِل (worker), عَمَل (work), and عُمّال (workers).
The اِستِفعال pattern as a whole also marks this as a verbal noun (مصدر), turning the action 'to use' into a noun: 'the using' or 'use'.

words.exampleTranslation: Using the phone while driving is dangerous.

parts.homeLangDetails: A triliteral root verb س-أ-ل in the base (Form I) past tense, third person masculine singular. The root carries the core meaning of asking or questioning. Almost all Arabic verbs in their citation form appear this way — as 'he [did]'.

words.translationLong: he asked; to ask, to question
words.exampleTranslation: The student asked the teacher about the homework.

parts.homeLangDetails: A verbal noun (مصدر) derived from the root س-أ-ل via the فُعال pattern. It nominalizes the act of asking into a noun meaning 'a question' or 'the act of asking'. The same root gives سَأَلَ (he asked) and أَسئِلة (questions).

words.exampleTranslation: This is a difficult question.

parts.homeLangDetails: The broken plural of سُؤال, formed by rearranging the root س-أ-ل into the أَفعِلة plural pattern. Arabic broken plurals work by fitting the root consonants into a new vowel pattern rather than adding a suffix, so the connection to سُؤال is through the shared root, not a visible suffix.

words.exampleSentence: لديَّ أَسئِلة كثيرة عن هذا الموضوع.
words.exampleTranslation: I have many questions about this topic.

parts.homeLangDetails: The مَ- prefix on a فَعلة pattern creates a noun indicating a concrete instance or matter arising from the root's action. Here, from س-أ-ل (to ask), it gives 'a matter that is asked about' — an issue, a problem, a case.
The root س-أ-ل, meaning to ask or question. The same root underlies سَأَلَ (he asked), سُؤال (a question), and أَسئِلة (questions).
The تاء مربوطة (ة) marks this as feminine and, in combination with the مَفعَلة pattern, signals a single instance or concrete matter — something you can point to and say 'this is the issue.'

words.translationLong: issue, matter, problem, question (in a formal or academic sense)
words.exampleSentence: هذه مَسألة معقّدة تحتاج إلى دراسة.
words.exampleTranslation: This is a complicated matter that needs study.

parts.homeLangDetails: A Form II verb (فَعَّلَ pattern) from the root ك-ل-م. The doubled middle letter (لّ) is characteristic of Form II, which here gives a transitive, directed sense: not just speaking in general, but speaking TO someone. Compare كَلام (speech) from the same root.

words.translationShort: to speak to, to talk to
words.translationLong: he spoke to; to speak to, to address someone
words.exampleTranslation: Ahmed spoke to his friend on the phone.

parts.homeLangDetails: A verbal noun (مصدر) from the root ك-ل-م via the فَعال pattern, indicating the general act or substance of speaking. It covers speech, talk, words, and discourse. The same root gives كَلَّمَ (to speak to), كَلِمة (a word), and مُتَكَلِّم (speaker).

words.exampleTranslation: His speech is clear and understandable.

parts.homeLangDetails: The root ك-ل-م in the فَعِل pattern, pointing to a single unit of speech. This form is closely related to كَلام (speech in general) but the pattern and the feminine suffix ة narrow it to one discrete word.
The تاء مربوطة (ة) here marks both the feminine gender and, crucially, a single concrete instance of speech — one word, as opposed to كَلام which is speech in the abstract.

words.exampleTranslation: I learned a new word today.

parts.homeLangDetails: The root ك-ل-م, the same base as in كَلِمة (word). Here the root appears without the singular feminine suffix, ready to take a plural ending.
The suffix ـات is a regular sound feminine plural in Arabic, meaning 'more than one of this thing'. It is added to كَلِمة (dropping the ة) to give كَلِمات: words.

words.exampleTranslation: Some Arabic words are difficult to pronounce.

parts.homeLangDetails: The prefix مُتَ- marks this as an active participle of a Form V verb (تَفَعَّلَ). Form V often gives reflexive or self-directed meaning to a root. Here it creates 'one who engages in speaking' — an active, ongoing speaker.
The doubled ل (لّ) reflects the Form V derivation from the root ك-ل-م. The same root runs through كَلام (speech), كَلِمة (word), and كَلَّمَ (to speak to someone).
The third root letter م of ك-ل-م, completing the participial form. Together مُتَكَلِّم means 'one who is speaking' or 'a speaker'.

words.translationLong: speaker, one who speaks, first person (in grammar)
words.exampleSentence: المُتَكَلِّم في المؤتمر كان واضحاً وصريحاً.
words.exampleTranslation: The speaker at the conference was clear and direct.

parts.homeLangDetails: A Form I triliteral root verb خ-ر-ج in the past tense, third person masculine singular. The root carries the core meaning of going out, exiting, or leaving. The same root gives خُروج (exit/departure), خارِج (outside), and مَخرَج (a way out).

words.translationLong: he went out; to go out, to exit, to leave
words.exampleTranslation: The children went out to the garden.

parts.homeLangDetails: A verbal noun (مصدر) from the root خ-ر-ج via the فُعول pattern, turning the action of going out into a noun. It can mean the act of exiting, a departure, or an exit (as a noun of action). The same root gives خَرَجَ (he went out), خارِج (outside), and مَخرَج (a way out).

words.exampleSentence: مُنِعَ خُروجُ السيارات من هذا الطريق.
words.exampleTranslation: Cars were prohibited from exiting via this road.

parts.homeLangDetails: An active participle of the root خ-ر-ج in the فاعِل pattern, literally 'one who is going out' or 'that which is outside'. In Arabic, active participles often double as adjectives or prepositions of place; خارِج functions as both 'outside' (preposition/adverb) and 'exterior' (adjective). The same root gives خَرَجَ (he went out) and خُروج (exit).

words.exampleTranslation: The children are playing outside the house.

parts.homeLangDetails: The prefix مَ- in the مَفعَل pattern creates a noun of place or instrument — 'the place where X happens' or 'the means by which X is done'. Applied to the root خ-ر-ج (to go out), it gives مَخرَج: the place or means of going out, i.e., an exit or a way out.
The root خ-ر-ج, the same root as in خَرَجَ (he went out), خُروج (departure), and خارِج (outside/exterior). All share the fundamental idea of going out or leaving.

words.translationLong: exit, way out, outlet; point of articulation (in phonetics)
words.exampleSentence: أين مَخرَج الطوارئ في هذا المبنى؟
words.exampleTranslation: Where is the emergency exit in this building?

parts.homeLangDetails: The three-consonant root د-خ-ل (d-kh-l) carries the core idea of entering or going inside. All the words in this set are built from this same root by changing the vowel pattern around it.
The vowel pattern a-a-a (فَعَلَ) is the standard past-tense pattern for a basic verb in Arabic, meaning 'he did [action]'. The root consonants slot into this pattern like a frame.

words.exampleTranslation: The student entered the classroom.

parts.homeLangDetails: The root د-خ-ل (d-kh-l) means entering or going inside. Here it appears in a noun pattern that expresses the act or process of entering.
The pattern فُعول (fu'ul) is a common verbal noun pattern in Arabic, turning a verb's meaning into a noun: 'the act of entering.' It is similar to adding '-ing' or '-ance' in English.

words.translationLong: entry, the act of entering, admission
words.exampleTranslation: Entry is free for all visitors.

parts.homeLangDetails: The root د-خ-ل (d-kh-l) means entering or going inside.

The pattern فاعِل (fā'il) forms the active participle in Arabic — the equivalent of '-ing' as an adjective, or sometimes a noun meaning 'one who does.' Here it means 'entering' or, more commonly, 'inside/interior.'

words.exampleTranslation: The book is inside the bag.

parts.homeLangDetails: The prefix م (m-) on a verb root creates a noun of place or instrument — it signals 'the place where the action happens.' It is the Arabic equivalent of adding '-way', '-place', or '-ery' to a verb in English.
The root د-خ-ل (d-kh-l) means entering or going inside. Combined with the م prefix and the مَفعَل vowel pattern, it becomes 'the place of entering.'

words.exampleTranslation: I waited for my friend at the entrance.

parts.homeLangDetails: The three-consonant root ج-ل-س (j-l-s) carries the core idea of sitting or being seated. All the words in this set are built from this same root.
The vowel pattern a-a-a (فَعَلَ) is the standard past-tense pattern for a basic Arabic verb, meaning 'he did [action].'

words.exampleTranslation: The father sat on the chair.

parts.homeLangDetails: The root ج-ل-س (j-l-s) means sitting or being seated.
The pattern فُعول (fu'ul) turns the verb into a noun expressing the act or state: 'the act of sitting, being seated.'

words.translationLong: sitting, the act of sitting down
words.exampleSentence: يُفضِّلُ المعلمُ جُلوسَ الطلابِ في صفوفٍ منتظمةٍ.
words.exampleTranslation: The teacher prefers the students sitting in orderly rows.

parts.homeLangDetails: The root ج-ل-س (j-l-s) means sitting or being seated.
The pattern فاعِل forms the active participle — here meaning 'one who is sitting' or simply 'seated.'

words.translationLong: sitting, seated, one who is sitting
words.exampleTranslation: I saw a man sitting in front of the store.

parts.homeLangDetails: The م (m-) prefix with the مَفعِل vowel pattern creates a noun of place — 'the place where sitting happens.' In many cultures this became the word for a formal gathering place or council chamber.
The root ج-ل-س (j-l-s) means sitting. A مَجلِس is literally 'a place of sitting,' which extended to mean a sitting session, assembly, or council.

words.exampleTranslation: The council of ministers convened this morning.

parts.homeLangDetails: The three-consonant root و-ص-ل (w-ṣ-l) carries a dual idea: reaching/arriving at something and connecting or joining things together. Both senses are present across the derived forms in this set.
The vowel pattern a-a-a (فَعَلَ) is the standard past-tense pattern for a basic Arabic verb.

words.translationLong: he arrived, he reached, he connected, he joined
words.exampleTranslation: The train arrived on time.

parts.homeLangDetails: The root و-ص-ل (w-ṣ-l) covers arriving and connecting.
The pattern فُعول turns the root into a verbal noun: 'the act of arriving' or 'arrival.' It can also mean access (the ability to connect or reach something).

words.translationLong: arrival, the act of reaching, access
words.exampleTranslation: His arrival at the airport was early.

parts.homeLangDetails: The root و-ص-ل (w-ṣ-l) covers arriving and connecting.
The pattern فاعِل forms the active participle — 'one who connects' or 'one who is arriving/reaching.' It can be used as an adjective or noun depending on context.

words.translationLong: one who connects, one who is arriving, connector
words.exampleSentence: الحرفُ الواصِلُ بينَ الكلمتينِ هو الواو.
words.exampleTranslation: The connecting letter between the two words is the waw.

parts.homeLangDetails: The م (m-) prefix here forms a noun of place or instrument — 'the place or means of connecting.' In electrical contexts it becomes 'conductor'; as a place name, Mosul (مَوصِل) was historically a key junction city.
The root و-ص-ل (w-ṣ-l) covers connecting and arriving. Combined with the م prefix, it yields 'a connector' or 'a place of joining.'

words.exampleTranslation: Copper is a good conductor of electricity.

parts.homeLangDetails: This is the verbal noun prefix pattern for Form VIII (افتعال) in Arabic. Form VIII adds a reflexive or mutual dimension to the root — here, not just connecting but connecting with each other, reaching out to someone. The doubled ت (tt) is characteristic of this form when the root begins with و.
The root و-ص-ل (w-ṣ-l) covers connecting and joining. In Form VIII, اتّصل means 'to communicate' or 'to get in touch with' — a mutual or directed act of connecting.

The suffix ال- (here -āl) is the ending of the اِفتِعال verbal noun pattern, which turns a Form VIII verb into a noun meaning 'the act of mutual/intensive connecting' — i.e., communication or contact.

words.exampleSentence: تلقيتُ اِتِّصالًا هاتفيًّا من صديقٍ قديمٍ.
words.exampleTranslation: I received a phone call from an old friend.

parts.homeLangDetails: The three-consonant root ف-ت-ح (f-t-ḥ) carries the idea of opening, unlocking, or conquering — all senses of making something accessible that was previously closed or shut.
The vowel pattern a-a-a (فَعَلَ) is the standard past-tense pattern for a basic Arabic verb.

words.translationLong: he opened, he unlocked, he conquered
words.exampleSentence: فَتَحَ الولدُ النافذةَ لأنَّ الغرفةَ كانت حارةً.
words.exampleTranslation: The boy opened the window because the room was hot.

parts.homeLangDetails: The root ف-ت-ح (f-t-ḥ) means opening or making accessible. As a bare verbal noun (masdar), فَتح refers both to the act of opening and, historically, to conquest or 'the opening up' of new lands. It is also the name of the short vowel mark (ـَ) in Arabic script, since it 'opens' the mouth.
The pattern فَعل (with a short vowel on the first consonant and a sukun on the second) is one of the simplest verbal noun patterns in Arabic, expressing the bare act or result of the verb.

words.translationLong: opening, conquest, the fatha vowel mark
words.exampleSentence: كانَ فَتحُ مكةَ حدثًا تاريخيًّا عظيمًا.
words.exampleTranslation: The conquest of Mecca was a great historical event.

parts.homeLangDetails: The Arabic root ف-ت-ح (f-t-ḥ) carries the core sense of opening, conquering, or beginning. The vowel pattern here (fāʿil) is the active participle pattern — the one who does the action.
The pattern فاعِل (fāʿil) is Arabic's standard active participle mold: it slots the root consonants in to mean 'the one who does X.' So فاتِح = the one who opens/conquers.

words.translationLong: one who opens; conqueror; light (as a shade of color)
words.exampleTranslation: Saladin was a great conqueror.

parts.homeLangDetails: The root ف-ت-ح (f-t-ḥ) meaning to open or conquer. Here it appears in the passive participle pattern, indicating something that has been opened.
The pattern مَفعول (mafʿūl) is Arabic's standard passive participle mold: 'that which has been done to.' Slot in the root and you get 'that which has been opened.'

words.translationLong: open; opened; (of a vowel) pronounced with an open mouth
words.exampleTranslation: The window is open all day.

parts.homeLangDetails: The root ف-ت-ح (f-t-ḥ) meaning to open. Here it appears in the instrument noun pattern, giving the thing used to open.

The pattern مِفعال (mifʿāl) is one of Arabic's instrument noun patterns — it names the tool or means by which the root action is carried out. So مِفتاح is literally 'the thing you use to open,' i.e., a key.

words.exampleTranslation: I forgot the house key in the car.

parts.homeLangDetails: The prefix أَ (a-) marks Form IV of the Arabic verb, which typically gives a causative meaning — 'to cause something to happen.' Here it takes the root غ-ل-ق (close/lock) and makes it transitive: to cause to be closed.
The root غ-ل-ق (gh-l-q) carries the core meaning of closing, locking, or shutting. It is the counterpart of ف-ت-ح (open).
The short vowel pattern a-a (fatḥa-fatḥa) on the root consonants signals past tense in Form IV: أَفعَلَ (afʿala). The final -a is the third-person masculine singular past ending.

words.translationLong: to close, to shut, to lock (Form IV past tense, 3rd person masculine singular)
words.exampleTranslation: The manager closed his office door.

parts.homeLangDetails: The prefix إِ (i-) here marks the verbal noun (maṣdar) of Form IV. Just as أَ- marks the Form IV verb, إِ- introduces its corresponding noun of action.
The pattern إِفعال (ifʿāl) is the standard verbal noun mold for Form IV verbs. It turns the verb 'to close' into the abstract noun 'closing' or 'closure.'

words.exampleSentence: أعلنت الشركة إِغلاق فروعها في المدينة.
words.exampleTranslation: The company announced the closure of its branches in the city.

parts.homeLangDetails: The pattern مُفعَل (mufʿal) is the passive participle of Form IV verbs. It means 'that which has been caused to be X' — so مُغلَق is 'that which has been closed/locked.'

words.exampleSentence: المحل مُغلَق في أيام العطل الرسمية.
words.exampleTranslation: The shop is closed on official holidays.

parts.homeLangDetails: The root ن-ظ-ر (n-ẓ-r) carries the core meaning of looking, seeing, or considering. It is a highly productive root that generates many words related to sight and perspective.
The short vowel pattern a-a-a (three fatḥas) on the root consonants, with the final -a as the third-person masculine singular marker, signals simple past tense Form I: فَعَلَ (faʿala).

words.translationLong: to look, to see, to consider (Form I past tense, 3rd person masculine singular)
words.exampleTranslation: The child looked at the sky in amazement.

parts.homeLangDetails: The pattern فَعَل (faʿal) is one of the common verbal noun patterns in Form I Arabic. It transforms the act of looking into an abstract noun: 'sight,' 'vision,' or 'consideration.'

words.exampleTranslation: In my view, this decision is correct.

parts.homeLangDetails: The pattern فاعِل (fāʿil) slots the root into the 'one who does X' mold. So ناظِر is 'one who looks' — a viewer, observer, or overseer.

words.translationLong: one who looks; observer; superintendent; director (archaic/formal)
words.exampleSentence: كان ناظِر المدرسة صارمًا لكن عادلًا.
words.exampleTranslation: The school principal was strict but fair.

parts.homeLangDetails: The pattern مَفعول (mafʿūl) means 'that which has been looked at' or, more abstractly, 'that through which things are seen.' مَنظور can mean 'visible' or, as a noun, 'perspective/point of view.'

words.translationLong: perspective, viewpoint; visible; (that which is looked at)
words.exampleSentence: يختلف الحكمُ على الأمور بحسب المَنظور الذي تنظر منه.
words.exampleTranslation: Judging matters differs according to the perspective from which you view them.

parts.homeLangDetails: The pattern مَفعَل (mafʿal) is a noun-of-place or noun-of-instance pattern in Arabic — it names the place where an action occurs or a concrete instance of it. مَنظَر is thus 'the place/thing that is looked at,' giving us 'scene' or 'view.'

words.exampleSentence: كان مَنظَر الغروب من الجبل رائعًا.
words.exampleTranslation: The view of the sunset from the mountain was magnificent.

parts.homeLangDetails: The root أ-ك-ل (ʾ-k-l) carries the core meaning of eating or consuming. It is one of the most basic roots in Arabic and generates a rich family of food-related words.
The short vowel pattern a-a-a on the root consonants with the final -a as third-person masculine singular marker signals simple past tense Form I: فَعَلَ (faʿala).

words.translationLong: to eat, to consume (Form I past tense, 3rd person masculine singular)
words.exampleTranslation: The children ate dinner quickly.

parts.homeLangDetails: The pattern فَعَل (faʿal) here forms the verbal noun — the abstract noun of the eating action — giving us 'food' or 'eating' as a concept.

words.translationLong: food; the act of eating; meal
words.exampleTranslation: Healthy food is important for a sound body.

parts.homeLangDetails: The pattern فاعِل (fāʿil) creates 'the one who eats.' Note that when the first root letter is a hamza (أ), the long ā of the pattern merges with it to produce the spelling آكِل rather than أاكِل.

words.exampleSentence: النمل آكِلُ النمل من أغرب المخلوقات.
words.exampleTranslation: The anteater is one of the strangest creatures.

parts.homeLangDetails: The pattern مَفعول (mafʿūl) means 'that which has been eaten' or more generally 'edible, fit to be eaten.' Used as a noun, مَأكول means food or foodstuff.

words.translationLong: food; edible; that which is eaten; foodstuff
words.exampleSentence: قدّمت المضيفة أنواعًا كثيرة من المَأكولات.
words.exampleTranslation: The hostess served many kinds of food.

parts.homeLangDetails: The prefix مَ/مِ is a place-of-action pattern in Arabic morphology. Attached to a root, it forms a noun meaning 'the place where this action happens.' So مَطعَم is literally 'the place of eating.'

words.exampleTranslation: We went to a nice restaurant yesterday.

parts.homeLangDetails: This is the bare three-letter root ش-ر-ب in the basic past tense (فَعَلَ) pattern, meaning 'he drank.' The vowels on the root letters carry the grammatical meaning — the root itself is the word.

words.translationLong: he drank, he used to drink
words.exampleTranslation: The boy drank a cup of water.

parts.homeLangDetails: The root ش-ر-ب placed in the فُعل vowel pattern forms a verbal noun (مصدر) meaning the act of drinking. The short vowel pattern alone transforms the root into an abstract noun — no extra prefix or suffix needed.

words.exampleTranslation: Drinking is necessary for health.

parts.homeLangDetails: The فاعِل pattern is the standard Arabic active participle, equivalent to an English '-er' or '-ing' agent noun. شارِب means 'one who drinks' or simply 'drinker.' The same pattern gives كاتِب (writer), سافِر (traveler), and so on. Note that شارِب also separately means 'mustache' in Arabic — the hairs that 'drink' or hang over the mouth.

words.exampleTranslation: He is an enthusiastic coffee drinker.

parts.homeLangDetails: The مَفعول pattern in Arabic forms passive participles — 'that which is verb-ed.' So مَشروب means literally 'that which is drunk,' i.e., a drink or beverage. The same pattern gives مَكتوب (written / a letter) and مَحمول (carried / mobile).

words.translationLong: a drink, a beverage, something drunk
words.exampleTranslation: What is your favorite drink?

parts.homeLangDetails: The مَفعول pattern marks 'that which is verb-ed.' See مَشروب.
The suffix ـات is the standard feminine sound plural in Arabic, added to many nouns (especially those from the مَفعول pattern) to form a regular plural. English equivalent: '-s'.

words.exampleTranslation: The waiter served the drinks to the guests.

parts.homeLangDetails: This is the root س-ف-ر placed in the فاعَلَ (Form III) verb pattern. Form III often conveys doing something with another party or doing something intensively/repeatedly. Here سافَرَ means 'he traveled' — the Form III adds a sense of purposeful journeying compared to the bare root.

words.translationLong: he traveled, he went on a journey
words.exampleSentence: سافَرَ أخي إلى فَرَنسا الصَّيفَ الماضي.
words.exampleTranslation: My brother traveled to France last summer.

parts.homeLangDetails: The root س-ف-ر in the فَعَل vowel pattern forms a verbal noun meaning 'travel' or 'a journey.' This is among the simplest noun forms — the root with a short-a on the first consonant and a short-a on the second.

words.exampleTranslation: Travel broadens the horizons.

parts.homeLangDetails: The prefix مُـ is characteristic of Form III active participles (مُفاعِل pattern). It marks 'the one who is doing the action.' مُسافِر literally means 'one who is traveling' — a traveler.

words.exampleTranslation: The traveler needs a passport.

parts.homeLangDetails: The suffix ـة (ta marbuta) here doesn't just mark feminine gender — it forms an institutional or collective noun from the root, in the فَعالة pattern. This pattern is common for professions and institutions: سِفارة (embassy), تِجارة (trade/commerce), زِراعة (agriculture). Think of it as '-ery' or '-age' in English — it turns an activity into a place or institution associated with it.

words.exampleSentence: ذَهَبتُ إلى السَّفارةِ لِتَجديدِ جَوازِ سَفَري.
words.exampleTranslation: I went to the embassy to renew my passport.

parts.homeLangDetails: The root ح-م-ل in the basic فَعَلَ past tense pattern means 'he carried' or 'he bore.' This root is remarkably productive: it gives us حِمل (a load), حامِل (carrier), مَحمول (carried/mobile), and even حَمل (pregnancy) — all the senses of 'bearing' something.

words.translationLong: he carried, he bore, he transported
words.exampleTranslation: The man carried the bag in his hand.

parts.homeLangDetails: The root ح-م-ل in the فِعل vowel pattern forms a noun for a concrete instance of the action — here, 'a load' or 'a burden.' The short-i on the first consonant is characteristic of this result-noun pattern.

words.exampleTranslation: The load was very heavy.

parts.homeLangDetails: The فاعِل pattern is the active participle — 'one who carries.' حامِل means 'carrier' or 'bearer,' and also 'pregnant' (one who bears a child). The same pattern gives شارِب (drinker), مُسافِر's underlying form, and countless others.

words.translationLong: carrier, bearer; pregnant (when referring to a woman)
words.exampleTranslation: He is the holder of a university degree.

parts.homeLangDetails: The مَفعول pattern marks 'that which is carried/verb-ed.' مَحمول literally means 'that which is carried.' Because mobile phones are carried in your hand or pocket, the word came to mean 'mobile phone' in modern Arabic. The same pattern gives مَشروب (drink) and مَكتوب (letter/written thing).

words.exampleTranslation: I forgot my mobile phone at home.

parts.homeLangDetails: The root ط-ل-ب in the basic فَعَلَ pattern means 'he requested,' 'he asked for,' or 'he sought.' This root underpins a whole family of words around requesting and seeking — from طَلَب (a request) to طالِب (a student, literally 'one who seeks knowledge').

words.translationLong: he requested, he asked for, he ordered (food/items)
words.exampleTranslation: The customer ordered a cup of coffee.

parts.homeLangDetails: This is the bare three-letter root ط-ل-ب in its simplest noun/verb form, meaning the act of seeking or requesting. The root is the foundation for all the related words in this set.

words.exampleTranslation: He submitted an application to the university.

parts.homeLangDetails: The فاعِل (fāʿil) pattern in Arabic forms an active participle — the one who does the action. The long vowel ā after the first root letter is the signature of this pattern.
The final root letter, completing the فاعِل pattern from the root ط-ل-ب. Together the pattern means 'one who seeks' — a seeker, and by extension, a student.

words.translationLong: student; one who seeks or requests
words.exampleTranslation: The student studies at the university.

parts.homeLangDetails: The مَفعول (mafʿūl) pattern is Arabic's standard way of forming a passive participle — something that has been acted upon. The مَ prefix is its opening marker.
The root ط-ل-ب fitted into the مَفعول pattern, with the characteristic long ū vowel in the middle. Together: 'that which is sought or requested.'

words.translationLong: required, wanted, sought after, in demand
words.exampleSentence: هذا المُؤَهَّلُ مَطلوبٌ في سوقِ العَمَل.
words.exampleTranslation: This qualification is in demand in the job market.

parts.homeLangDetails: The مُ prefix signals a Form V active participle. Form V (تَفَعَّل) typically adds reflexive or intensive force to the base meaning.
The root ط-ل-ب in Form V (تَطَلَّبَ), which intensifies the basic meaning of seeking into actively demanding or requiring something.
The feminine sound plural suffix, used here to make the active participle plural: مُتَطَلِّب (a requirement) → مُتَطَلِّبات (requirements).

words.exampleSentence: ما هي مُتَطَلِّباتُ القَبولِ في هذا البَرنامَج؟
words.exampleTranslation: What are the admission requirements for this program?

parts.homeLangDetails: This word is special: فَعَلَ is the canonical placeholder verb Arabic grammarians use to illustrate verb patterns, equivalent to the English 'to do.' It is also a real verb meaning 'he did' or 'he acted.' The root ف-ع-ل is the abstract template root used in Arabic linguistics.

words.translationLong: he did, he acted, he performed
words.exampleTranslation: The boy did his homework.

parts.homeLangDetails: The pattern فِعل (with a kasra under the first letter) is a common Arabic noun pattern (مصدر-like or noun of instance) that names the concept itself: not the act of doing, but the thing done, or the general concept of action. In grammar, فِعل specifically means 'verb.'

words.exampleTranslation: The verb is the foundation of the verbal sentence.

parts.homeLangDetails: The فاعِل pattern (active participle) applied to the root ف-ع-ل. The long ā vowel after the first root letter is the hallmark of this pattern.
The final root letter completing the فاعِل pattern: 'one who acts/does.' In grammar, فاعِل is also the technical term for the grammatical subject.

words.translationLong: doer, agent, actor; (grammatically) subject of a verb
words.exampleSentence: الفاعِلُ في الجُملةِ هو مَن يَقومُ بِالفِعل.
words.exampleTranslation: The subject in the sentence is the one who performs the action.

parts.homeLangDetails: Opening marker of the مَفعول pattern, which forms passive participles — things acted upon. Fittingly, مَفعول is itself the very word Arabic grammar uses for 'direct object' (the thing acted upon).
The root ف-ع-ل fitted into the مَفعول passive participle pattern. The long ū vowel in the middle is characteristic of this pattern.

words.translationLong: that which is done; (grammatically) direct object
words.exampleSentence: المَفعولُ بِهِ يَقَعُ عَلَيهِ فِعلُ الفاعِل.
words.exampleTranslation: The direct object is what the subject's action falls upon.

parts.homeLangDetails: This initial أَ is part of the أَفعال broken plural pattern (أَفعَال), one of Arabic's many internal plural templates. Unlike English which adds -s, Arabic often reshapes the entire word to form a plural.
The final ل completes the أَفعَال broken plural template applied to the root ف-ع-ل. This same pattern (أَفعَال) is extremely common for pluralizing Arabic nouns and adjectives.

words.translationLong: acts, deeds, actions; (grammatically) verbs (plural of فِعل)
words.exampleTranslation: Your actions reflect your character.

parts.homeLangDetails: The bare root و-ج-د in its basic verb form (past tense, third person masculine singular): 'he found.' This root carries a dual sense of finding something and of existence itself, which is why it gives rise to words meaning both 'to find' and 'existence.'

words.exampleTranslation: The student found the book in the library.

parts.homeLangDetails: The pattern فُعول applied to the root و-ج-د forms a verbal noun (مصدر) meaning the abstract concept of existence or being present. The same root that means 'to find' gives us the noun for 'existence' — what is found, what is there.

words.exampleTranslation: Your presence here makes us happy.

parts.homeLangDetails: The مَفعول passive participle pattern applied to و-ج-د: 'that which is found' — i.e., something that exists, something present.

words.exampleTranslation: Is the book available in the library?

parts.homeLangDetails: From the root و-ج-ه meaning 'face' or 'direction.' The pattern وِجهة captures the sense of a direction one faces or heads toward. Note this is a different root (و-ج-ه) from وَجَدَ (و-ج-د), though they share the first two letters.
The tāʾ marbūṭa (ة), a feminine ending that here helps form a noun of instance or direction: not just 'facing' in the abstract, but a concrete direction or viewpoint.

words.exampleTranslation: What is your travel destination?

parts.homeLangDetails: The bare root ص-ن-ع in its basic verb form: 'he made' or 'he crafted.' This root specifically implies skilled making or manufacturing, with a sense of craftsmanship beyond mere doing.

words.translationLong: he made, he manufactured, he crafted
words.exampleTranslation: The blacksmith made a sword from iron.

parts.homeLangDetails: The root ص-ن-ع in the فِعالة pattern, which in Arabic typically forms nouns denoting a craft, trade, or profession. The same pattern gives طِباعة (printing), زِراعة (agriculture), تِجارة (trade).
The tāʾ marbūṭa (ة) completing the noun and marking it as feminine, as is typical for abstract nouns of craft or profession in Arabic.

words.exampleSentence: تَطَوَّرَتِ الصِّناعةُ بِشَكلٍ كَبيرٍ في القَرنِ العِشرين.
words.exampleTranslation: Industry developed greatly in the twentieth century.

parts.homeLangDetails: The root ذ-ه-ب (going, gold) here appears in the basic past-tense verb pattern (Form I, third person masculine singular). The whole word is a single inflected verb form — the root consonants carry the meaning of 'going' and the vowel pattern signals past tense and subject.

words.exampleTranslation: Ahmed went to school.

parts.homeLangDetails: The root ذ-ه-ب (going) in the فَعَال (faʿāl) verbal noun pattern. This pattern is common for verbs of motion and produces an abstract noun meaning 'the act of going.' You'll see the same pattern in كَلام (speech) from ك-ل-م.

words.translationLong: going, departure, one-way (as in a ticket)
words.exampleTranslation: I bought a round-trip ticket (literally: a ticket of going and return).

parts.homeLangDetails: The prefix مَ- here creates a noun of place or direction from the root ذ-ه-ب. Literally 'the place/direction one goes.' This same مَ- pattern appears in مَجلِس (sitting place, council) and مَدرَسَة (school, place of study).

words.translationLong: school of thought, religious or legal doctrine, direction, path
words.exampleTranslation: He belongs to the Hanafi school of thought.

parts.homeLangDetails: The root ذ-ه-ب in the active participle pattern فَاعِل (fāʿil). This pattern creates an adjective or noun meaning 'one who is going' or 'one who goes' — the equivalent of the English '-ing' form used as an adjective. Compare كاتِب (writer, one who writes) or جالِس (sitting).

words.translationLong: going, departing, one who is going
words.exampleTranslation: I am going to work now.

parts.homeLangDetails: The root ر-ج-ع (returning, going back) in the basic Form I past-tense pattern, third person masculine singular. The vowel pattern فَعَلَ signals past tense, and the root consonants carry the core meaning of returning.

words.translationLong: he returned, he went back, he came back
words.exampleTranslation: My father returned from the trip.

parts.homeLangDetails: The root ر-ج-ع in the verbal noun pattern فُعول (fuʿūl), which is common for Form I verbs of motion. This pattern produces an abstract noun meaning 'the act of returning.' Compare ذُهول, دُخول (entering), خُروج (exiting).

words.exampleTranslation: I hope for your quick return.

parts.homeLangDetails: The root ر-ج-ع in the active participle pattern فَاعِل (fāʿil), meaning 'one who is returning' or 'one who returns.' This same pattern gives ذاهِب (one going) and كاتِب (one writing). Context can also make it an imperative-like adjective meaning 'refer to' or 'check.'

words.translationLong: returning, one who returns; also used as 'see' or 'refer to' in written Arabic
words.exampleTranslation: He is returning from the trip tomorrow.

parts.homeLangDetails: This prefix turns a root into a place or tool word, and it's extremely productive. مَكْتَب (maktab) is "office" (place of writing), مَدْرَسَة (madrasa) is "school" (place of studying), مَطْبَخ (matbakh) is "kitchen" (place of cooking). Spot a م at the start and think "place where this root-action happens."

words.translationLong: reference, source, authority, point of return
words.exampleTranslation: This book is an important reference for researchers.

parts.homeLangDetails: The مُ- prefix here is part of the Form III verbal noun pattern مُفَاعَلَة (mufāʿala). Form III typically conveys a reciprocal or intensive action — doing something back and forth, or going over something repeatedly. This مُ- is not the same as the place/instrument prefix مَ- seen in مَرجِع or مَذهَب.

From the root ر-ج-ع, here appearing in the Form III stem. The Form III pattern مُفَاعَلَة adds a sense of thoroughness or repetition — 'going back over' something carefully. So مُراجَعة is not just a return but a going-back-over, a review.

The -ة ending here is the standard feminine suffix that completes the مُفَاعَلَة verbal noun pattern. It marks this as an abstract noun (the act of reviewing/revising) rather than a participle or adjective.

words.translationLong: review, revision, proofreading, audit, going over something again
words.exampleTranslation: I spent the night reviewing my lessons.

parts.homeLangDetails: The root ح-ب-ب (love, liking) in the Form I past tense. The doubled middle and final consonant (ب-ب) is a feature of 'doubled roots' (roots where the second and third consonants are the same), and they merge into a single written consonant with a shadda (ّ). The full root consonants are ح-ب-ب.

words.translationLong: he loved, he liked, he was fond of
words.exampleTranslation: The child loved playing in the garden.

parts.homeLangDetails: The root ح-ب-ب in the verbal noun pattern فُعل (fuʿl), which is typical for doubled roots. The two ب letters merge with a shadda. This short, punchy pattern is common for deep, essential nouns — compare with حُكم (rule/judgment) or حُزن (sadness).

words.exampleTranslation: Love is the foundation of life.

parts.homeLangDetails: The root ح-ب-ب in the pattern فَعيل (faʿīl), which commonly forms adjectives meaning 'one who is [quality]' or passive-like adjectives. Here it means 'one who is loved' or 'beloved.' The same pattern appears in كَريم (generous), جَميل (beautiful), and قَريب (near).

words.exampleTranslation: You are my dear one, my friend.

parts.homeLangDetails: The مَ- here is part of the Form I passive participle pattern مَفعول (mafʿūl), not the place/instrument prefix. It attaches to a root to mean 'that which is [verb]ed' — so مَحبوب = 'that which is loved.' This pattern is very productive: مَكتوب (written), مَفهوم (understood), مَعلوم (known).

From the root ح-ب-ب in the مَفعول passive participle pattern. The doubled ب of the root appears as بوب in this pattern. Together with مَ-, the whole word means 'the one who is loved' or simply 'loved, popular.'

words.exampleTranslation: He is a beloved teacher among his students.

parts.homeLangDetails: The plural of حَبيب (beloved), formed on the pattern أَفعال (afʿāl) — a very common broken plural pattern in Arabic. 'Broken plurals' rearrange the vowels around the root consonants rather than adding a suffix, so حَبيب becomes أَحباب. Compare كِتاب → أَكتاب, صاحِب → أَصحاب.

words.translationLong: loved ones, dear ones, beloveds, friends
words.exampleTranslation: We gathered with our loved ones during the holiday.

parts.homeLangDetails: The root ق-و-ل (saying, speech) in the Form I past tense, third person masculine singular. This is a 'hollow verb' — the middle root letter is a weak letter (و), which disappears in many conjugations and is replaced by a long vowel. The underlying root ق-و-ل is visible in related words like قَول (saying) and أَقوال (sayings).

words.translationLong: he said, he spoke, he stated
words.exampleTranslation: The teacher said that the lesson is important.

parts.homeLangDetails: The root ق-و-ل in its base noun pattern. The root's middle letter 'و' carries the vowel meaning of the saying/speech action.
The final radical of the root ق-و-ل. Together the three letters form the basic verbal noun pattern (فَعْل) meaning 'a saying' or 'a word.'

words.exampleTranslation: This is a wise saying.
words.flag: only 1 part — the word is a single root-based unit (ق-و-ل in فَعْل pattern) and does not split into distinct morpheme parts beyond the root itself

parts.homeLangDetails: From the root ق-و-ل, here in the active participle pattern (فَاعِل). The 'و' shifts to a hamza (ء) as part of regular Arabic phonology when forming this pattern.
The -il ending completes the فَاعِل active participle pattern, meaning 'one who does the action.' Equivalent to the English agentive suffix -er (speaker, sayer).

words.exampleTranslation: Who is the one who said these words?

parts.homeLangDetails: The مَـ prefix here creates a passive participle (مَفْعُول pattern), meaning 'that which is said' — something spoken or quoted. Compare مَكتوب (written) from the same pattern.

words.translationLong: that which is said, a quote, predicate (in grammar)
words.exampleTranslation: The predicate in the sentence is the new information stated.

parts.homeLangDetails: The root ق-و-ل in the أَفْعَال broken plural pattern. Arabic often forms plurals by restructuring the internal vowels and adding a prefix rather than a suffix — this is called a 'broken plural.'
The final letter closing out the أَفْعَال broken plural pattern. The plural of قَول (a saying) becomes أَقوال (sayings/statements).

words.exampleTranslation: The sayings of the wise remain forever.

parts.homeLangDetails: The مَـ prefix combined with the feminine ending creates a noun of instance or concept — here, 'a saying' or 'a maxim' as a packaged idea. Slightly different from the plain passive participle مَقول; the feminine marker gives it a more noun-like, categorical feel.

The tā' marbūṭa (ة) here doesn't just mark grammatical gender — it nominalizes the passive participle into a concrete noun meaning 'a maxim' or 'a dictum,' a thing that was said and has become a fixed expression.

words.exampleTranslation: This is a famous saying about patience.

parts.homeLangDetails: The تَـ prefix signals Pattern V (تَفَعَّلَ), the reflexive or intensive-reflexive form. Here it means 'to engage oneself in speaking' — actively and fully speaking, not just uttering a word.

From the root ك-ل-م, here with the doubled middle radical (Pattern II: كَلَّمَ = to speak to someone). Pattern V (تَكَلَّمَ) reflexivizes this, turning 'speaking at someone' into 'speaking' as a self-directed activity.

words.translationLong: he spoke, he talked, he conversed
words.exampleTranslation: The man spoke with a clear voice.

parts.homeLangDetails: When you see تَ- prefixed onto a verb pattern, it often signals a reflexive or middle-voice meaning, similar to adding "-self" in English. تَعَلَّمَ (ta'allama) means "he learned" (reflexively acquiring knowledge), versus عَلَّمَ (he taught someone else). It's a clue that the subject is acting on or for itself.
The root ك-ل-م in the verbal noun (masdar) of Pattern V. The doubled middle radical is preserved, and the pattern تَفَعُّل produces an abstract noun of the ongoing or reflexive action.
The final radical completing the تَفَعُّل verbal noun pattern. The full form تَكَلُّم means 'the act of speaking' as an abstract or ongoing process.

words.translationLong: the act of speaking, speech, verbal expression
words.exampleTranslation: Speaking clearly is an important skill.

parts.homeLangDetails: The مُتَـ prefix marks the active participle of Pattern V (مُتَفَعِّل). It means 'one who is engaged in the reflexive/intensive action' — here, someone actively speaking.
The root ك-ل-م with the doubled middle radical, in the active participle pattern of Form V. The vowel pattern -ِّ (kasra + shadda) is characteristic of مُتَفَعِّل active participles.
Wait — this is actually the final radical م of the root ك-ل-م, closing the active participle form مُتَكَلِّم (speaker).

words.exampleTranslation: The Arabic speakers are many.
words.flag: restructured — the word مُتَكَلِّمون is best analyzed as مُتَكَلِّم (active participle of Pattern V from root ك-ل-م) + ون (masculine plural); internal root-pattern fusion makes clean 4-part splitting difficult

parts.homeLangDetails: When you see تَ- prefixed onto a verb pattern, it often signals a reflexive or middle-voice meaning, similar to adding "-self" in English. تَعَلَّمَ (ta'allama) means "he learned" (reflexively acquiring knowledge), versus عَلَّمَ (he taught someone else). It's a clue that the subject is acting on or for itself.

From the root ع-ل-م, here in Pattern II (عَلَّمَ = to teach). Pattern V reflexivizes this: تَعَلَّمَ means 'to teach oneself,' i.e., to learn. It is one of the most elegant reflexive formations in Arabic.

words.translationLong: he learned, he studied, he taught himself
words.exampleTranslation: The child learned to read quickly.

parts.homeLangDetails: When you see تَ- prefixed onto a verb pattern, it often signals a reflexive or middle-voice meaning, similar to adding "-self" in English. تَعَلَّمَ (ta'allama) means "he learned" (reflexively acquiring knowledge), versus عَلَّمَ (he taught someone else). It's a clue that the subject is acting on or for itself.
The root ع-ل-م with the doubled middle radical, in the verbal noun pattern of Pattern V (تَفَعُّل). The doubled ل is characteristic of the Form II root from which Pattern V derives.
The final radical م of the root ع-ل-م, completing the تَفَعُّل verbal noun. The full form تَعَلُّم means 'the act of learning.'

words.translationLong: learning, the act of learning, education
words.exampleTranslation: Learning languages opens new doors.

parts.homeLangDetails: The active participle of Pattern V from the root ع-ل-م, following the مُتَفَعِّل pattern. Literally 'one who is in the process of teaching himself,' i.e., a learner or educated person.

words.exampleTranslation: Learners always seek knowledge.
words.flag: only 2 clean parts — مُتَعَلِّم is a fused active participle form from Pattern V of root ع-ل-م and resists further splitting without misrepresenting the morphology

parts.homeLangDetails: The اِسْتَـ prefix signals Pattern X (اِستَفْعَلَ), the 'seeking' or causative-reflexive form. Here it means 'to put to use' or 'to seek the work/function of something' — i.e., to employ or use it.
From the root ع-م-ل (work, action, doing). In Pattern X, اِستَعمَلَ literally means 'to seek/employ the work of' something — hence 'to use' or 'to utilize.'

words.translationLong: he used, he employed, he utilized, he operated
words.exampleTranslation: The student used the dictionary to understand the words.

parts.homeLangDetails: The مُسْتَـ prefix marks the passive participle of Pattern X (مُستَفْعَل). Together with the root, it means 'that which has been put to use' — something used or second-hand.

words.exampleTranslation: He bought a used car at a reasonable price.

parts.homeLangDetails: The اِسْتِـ prefix in the verbal noun of Pattern X (اِستِفْعَال). The vowel shifts from اِستَ (verb) to اِستِ (verbal noun prefix) as part of the pattern.
The root ع-م-ل in the verbal noun body of Pattern X (اِستِفْعَال). The long ā vowel (اِستِعْمَال) is characteristic of this pattern's verbal noun.
The long ā that completes the اِستِفْعَال pattern. اِستِعمال means 'the act of using' or 'usage.' The plural is formed by adding ات.
The تـ of the ات feminine plural suffix, attached here to the verbal noun اِستِعمال to form اِستِعمالات (uses, usages, applications).

words.exampleSentence: لهذه الأداة اِستِعمالات كثيرة في المطبخ.
words.exampleTranslation: This tool has many uses in the kitchen.
words.flag: 5 parts if fully split (اِسْتِ + عَ + مَا + لَا + ت) — simplified to 4 by grouping the Pattern X verbal noun body together

parts.homeLangDetails: Wait — اِنتَظَرَ is actually Pattern VIII (اِفْتَعَلَ), not Pattern VII. The اِنْـ here is not the Pattern VII prefix (اِنْفَعَلَ) but rather اِـ (the epenthetic alif) + the first radical ن of the root ن-ظ-ر fusing with the Pattern VIII infix تَـ. The result looks like اِنتَ but is اِ + ن (root) + تَ (infix).
From the root ن-ظ-ر, meaning to look, to watch, to regard. In Pattern VIII (اِنتَظَرَ), the meaning shifts to 'to wait' — literally 'to hold oneself in a state of watching/looking out for something.'

words.translationLong: he waited, he awaited, he expected
words.exampleSentence: اِنتَظَرَ صديقه أمام المحطة لمدة ساعة.
words.exampleTranslation: He waited for his friend in front of the station for an hour.

parts.homeLangDetails: The اِنْ- prefix marks Pattern VII (انفعال), which typically gives a verb or verbal noun a passive or reflexive sense — something happening to the subject rather than being actively done by them. Here it frames 'waiting' as a state one falls into.

Part of the Pattern VIII (افتعال) verbal noun structure — the تِ here is the characteristic infix of Pattern VIII, sandwiched between the first and second root letters. It intensifies or internalizes the root meaning.
From the root ن-ظ-ر (to look, to see, to watch). Waiting is conceived as 'looking out for' something — watching and expecting. The same root gives نَظَر (sight, view) and نَاظِر (observer).

words.exampleTranslation: The wait for the results was long.
words.flag: morpheme boundaries in Arabic roots are interleaved and approximate; parts are presented for pedagogical clarity

parts.homeLangDetails: The مُ- prefix on a Pattern VIII form creates an active participle — the person doing the waiting. Combined with the Pattern VIII infix تَ, it yields مُنتَ- as the characteristic opening of these participles.
The تَ infix is the hallmark of Pattern VIII (افتعال). It appears between the first and second root consonants and adds an internalized or intensive nuance to the root action.
From the root ن-ظ-ر (to look, watch, observe). Waiting is 'watching for' something. The same root gives نَظَر (sight), نَاظِر (observer, overseer), and اِنتِظار (waiting).

words.translationLong: waiting, expectant, one who is waiting
words.exampleTranslation: He is waiting for his friend in front of the station.
words.flag: morpheme boundaries in Arabic roots are interleaved and approximate; parts are presented for pedagogical clarity

parts.homeLangDetails: The اِنْ- prefix marks Pattern VII, giving the verb a passive or reflexive character — the action happens to or within the subject. Here, 'ending' is something that overtakes the subject: it came to an end, rather than actively ending something else.
The تَ here is the Pattern VIII infix, though in some analyses اِنتَهى is treated as a mixed or assimilated form. It contributes the sense of an internal or completed process.
From the root ن-هـ-ي / ن-هـ-و meaning 'end, limit, boundary.' Related words include نِهَاية (end, conclusion) and مُنتَهى (furthest point, utmost). The root evokes a boundary or terminus.

words.translationLong: it came to an end, it was over, it concluded
words.exampleTranslation: The film ended before midnight.
words.flag: morpheme boundaries in Arabic roots are interleaved and approximate; parts are presented for pedagogical clarity

parts.homeLangDetails: The اِنْ- prefix marks Pattern VII, framing the verbal noun as describing a process that completes itself — something reaching its own end rather than being ended by an outside agent.

The تِ is the characteristic infix of Pattern VIII verbal nouns (مصدر افتعال), placing the action in the internal/intensive category. Its presence here reflects the assimilated form of the root's conjugation pattern.
From the root ن-هـ-ي, meaning 'end, limit.' The long vowel -اء is characteristic of this verbal noun pattern. Related: نِهَاية (ending), مُنتَهى (endpoint), اِنتَهى (it ended).

words.exampleSentence: تاريخ اِنتِهاء صلاحية جواز سفره قريب.
words.exampleTranslation: The expiry date of his passport is approaching.
words.flag: morpheme boundaries in Arabic roots are interleaved and approximate; parts are presented for pedagogical clarity

parts.homeLangDetails: The مُ- prefix combined with the Pattern VIII form creates an active participle — describing the subject as currently in the state of having ended or being finished. مُنتَ- is the standard opening for Pattern VIII active participles.
The تَ infix is the defining feature of Pattern VIII. It adds an internalized quality — the ending has happened from within or upon the subject itself.
From the root ن-هـ-ي (end, limit). The same root gives اِنتَهى (it ended), اِنتِهاء (ending/expiry), and نِهَاية (conclusion). Something مُنتَهي has run its course.

words.exampleTranslation: His contract has been expired for a month.
words.flag: morpheme boundaries in Arabic roots are interleaved and approximate; parts are presented for pedagogical clarity

parts.homeLangDetails: The اِسْتَ- prefix marks Pattern X (استفعال), which typically conveys seeking or requesting something, or deeming something to be a certain way. In اِستَقبَلَ, it carries the sense of actively positioning oneself to face or receive — orienting toward something.

From the root ق-ب-ل, meaning 'before, in front of, facing.' Related words include قَبْل (before), مُقابِل (opposite, facing), and قِبلة (the direction of prayer). The idea is of orientation toward something ahead.

The final -لَ completes the root ق-ب-ل. The past tense third-person masculine singular in Arabic is marked by the vowel pattern of the conjugated form rather than a distinct suffix. This is the bare past-tense form: 'he received/welcomed.'

words.translationLong: he received, he welcomed, he greeted, he faced
words.exampleTranslation: The director welcomed the guests at the entrance.
words.flag: morpheme boundaries in Arabic roots are interleaved and approximate; parts are presented for pedagogical clarity

parts.homeLangDetails: The اِسْتِ- here is the verbal noun form of the Pattern X prefix (استفعال). In this word, it captures the act of actively turning toward and receiving — the institutionalized gesture of welcome or reception.

From the root ق-ب-ل (before, in front of, facing). The long vowel -ا is part of the Pattern X verbal noun template (استفعال → استِفعال). Related: قَبْل (before), مُقابَلة (meeting, interview), اِستِقبال (reception).
The final ل completes the root ق-ب-ل and closes the verbal noun pattern. Together اِستِقبال is the مصدر (verbal noun) of اِستَقبَلَ, meaning the act or event of receiving and welcoming.

words.exampleTranslation: The reception party was wonderful.
words.flag: morpheme boundaries in Arabic roots are interleaved and approximate; parts are presented for pedagogical clarity

parts.homeLangDetails: The مُسْتَ- prefix marks a Pattern X participle. As a passive participle (مفعول pattern), مُستَقبَل means 'that which is received' or, by extension, 'that which lies ahead and is awaited.' This metaphorical leap — from 'received' to 'future' — is the heart of the word's meaning.

From the root ق-ب-ل (before, in front of, facing). The future is literally what is 'in front of' us, what we face and receive. The same root gives اِستَقبَلَ (to welcome), اِستِقبال (reception), and قِبلة (prayer direction).

The final ل completes the root ق-ب-ل and closes the passive participle pattern. As a passive participle, مُستَقبَل is 'that which will be received' — extended semantically to mean the future, what lies ahead waiting to be met.

words.exampleTranslation: I think a lot about the future.
words.flag: morpheme boundaries in Arabic roots are interleaved and approximate; parts are presented for pedagogical clarity

parts.homeLangDetails: The اِسْتَ- prefix marks Pattern X, which often conveys seeking or actively pursuing something. Here, اِستَمَعَ means to actively lend one's ear — to seek out and receive sound intentionally. It's a more deliberate act of listening than simply hearing.

From the root س-م-ع (to hear). The first root letter سـ is absorbed into the اِسْتَ- prefix cluster, so مَ represents the second root consonant in context. The root conveys receiving sound — hearing and listening.
The final ع completes the root س-م-ع. This is the third-person masculine singular past tense form, indicated by the vowel pattern of the conjugation. 'He listened (attentively).'

words.translationLong: he listened, he listened attentively, he paid attention
words.exampleTranslation: The students listened to the lecture attentively.
words.flag: morpheme boundaries in Arabic roots are interleaved and approximate; parts are presented for pedagogical clarity

parts.homeLangDetails: The اِسْتِ- is the verbal noun form of the Pattern X prefix. In اِستِماع, it captures the act of actively and intentionally listening — seeking out sound rather than passively hearing it.

From the root س-م-ع (hearing, listening). The long vowel ما is part of the Pattern X verbal noun template (استفعال → استِماع here with the ع root). Related: سَمِعَ (he heard), سَمَاع (hearing), مُستَمِع (listener).
The final ع completes the root س-م-ع and closes the verbal noun. اِستِماع is the مصدر of اِستَمَعَ — the act of attentive, deliberate listening.

words.exampleSentence: مهارة الاِستِماع مهمة في تعلم اللغات.
words.exampleTranslation: Listening skill is important in language learning.
words.flag: morpheme boundaries in Arabic roots are interleaved and approximate; parts are presented for pedagogical clarity

parts.homeLangDetails: The مُسْتَ- prefix creates an active participle from Pattern X — the person actively doing the seeking/receiving. A مُستَمِع is someone who is actively and attentively listening, not just passively hearing.

From the root س-م-ع (hearing, listening). In the active participle of Pattern X, the second root consonant carries a kasra (ِ). Related: اِستَمَعَ (he listened), اِستِماع (listening), سَمِعَ (he heard).
The final ع completes the root س-م-ع and closes the active participle form. مُستَمِع is 'one who listens' — an active, engaged listener.

words.translationLong: listener, audience member, one who listens
words.exampleTranslation: The listener was attentive throughout the programme.
words.flag: morpheme boundaries in Arabic roots are interleaved and approximate; parts are presented for pedagogical clarity

parts.homeLangDetails: The اِسْتَ- prefix marks Pattern X. Here it conveys seeking or achieving a state of rest — actively settling into stability. اِستَقَرَّ is not just being still, it is the act of coming to rest, of establishing oneself somewhere.

From the root ق-ر-ر, meaning 'to settle, to be firm, to be decided.' The doubling of the final root letter (رَّ) is characteristic of roots with a doubled third consonant (مضعّف). Related: قَرار (decision, settlement), مُستَقِرّ (stable), اِستِقرار (stability).

The doubled رَّ is both the second and third consonant of the root ق-ر-ر fused together, a common feature of doubled-root verbs in Arabic. The past tense third-person form ends here. 'He settled, he became stable.'

words.translationLong: he settled, he stabilized, he became established, he came to rest
words.exampleSentence: اِستَقَرَّ الوضع بعد فترة من التوتر.
words.exampleTranslation: The situation stabilized after a period of tension.
words.flag: morpheme boundaries in Arabic roots are interleaved and approximate; parts are presented for pedagogical clarity

parts.homeLangDetails: The اِسْتِ- is the verbal noun form of the Pattern X prefix. In اِستِقرار, it captures the process of achieving and maintaining stability — not just a static state, but the act of settling into one.

From the root ق-ر-ر (to settle, be firm, be decided). The long vowel -ا is part of the Pattern X verbal noun template. The doubled root consonant appears once in this verbal noun form. Related: قَرار (decision), مُستَقِرّ (stable), اِستَقَرَّ (he settled).
The final ر completes the doubled root ق-ر-ر and closes the verbal noun. اِستِقرار is the مصدر of اِستَقَرَّ — the state or process of stability and settlement.

words.exampleSentence: يسعى الجميع إلى الاِستِقرار في حياتهم.
words.exampleTranslation: Everyone strives for stability in their lives.
words.flag: morpheme boundaries in Arabic roots are interleaved and approximate; parts are presented for pedagogical clarity

parts.homeLangDetails: The مُسْتَ- prefix creates an active participle from Pattern X — the person or thing that has actively achieved a state of rest or stability. مُستَقِرّ describes something that has settled and remains so.

From the root ق-ر-ر (to settle, to be firm). In the active participle of Pattern X, the second root consonant carries a kasra (ِ). Related: اِستَقَرَّ (he settled), اِستِقرار (stability), قَرار (decision).

The doubled رّ is the fused second and third consonant of the root ق-ر-ر — characteristic of doubled-root (مضعّف) words. As an active participle, مُستَقِرّ means 'one who/that which is settled, stable.'

words.exampleTranslation: He lives in a stable and comfortable environment.
words.flag: morpheme boundaries in Arabic roots are interleaved and approximate; parts are presented for pedagogical clarity

parts.homeLangDetails: The اِنْ- prefix marks Pattern VII (انفعال), which gives the verb a passive or reflexive meaning — the action happens to the subject rather than being done by it. اِنكَسَرَ means not 'he broke something' but 'it broke' — the breaking happened to it. This is a very common pattern for expressing things that break, open, or transform on their own.

From the root ك-س-ر (to break, to fracture). The base verb كَسَرَ means 'he broke (something).' With اِنْ-, it becomes اِنكَسَرَ — 'it broke (by itself/passively).' Related: كَسْر (breaking, fracture), كَسِير (broken), مَكسور (broken, in grammar: carrying a kasra vowel).
The final رَ completes the root ك-س-ر. This is the third-person masculine singular past tense ending, marked by the vowel pattern of the conjugation. 'It broke.'

words.translationLong: it broke, it snapped, it fractured (on its own)
words.exampleSentence: اِنكَسَرَ الكوب حين سقط على الأرض.
words.exampleTranslation: The cup broke when it fell to the floor.
words.flag: morpheme boundaries in Arabic roots are interleaved and approximate; parts are presented for pedagogical clarity

parts.homeLangDetails: The اِن- prefix marks Pattern VII, which turns an active verb into a passive or reflexive one — something happening to the subject rather than being done by them. Here it takes كَسَرَ (to break something) and makes it 'to be broken' or 'to break apart on its own.'
From the root ك-س-ر (to break, to fracture). The verbal noun pattern فِعَال here captures the abstract result of the breaking action.

words.exampleTranslation: The soldier suffered a fracture in his arm.

parts.homeLangDetails: The مُن- here is the active participle prefix مُ- fused onto the اِن- of Pattern VII. Together they form the active participle of Pattern VII verbs, meaning 'one who/that is in a state of being broken' — broken, shattered, dejected.
From the root ك-س-ر (to break, to fracture). In the active participle of Pattern VII, the root appears in this vocalized form to indicate a state resulting from the breaking action.

words.exampleTranslation: He looked at me with a broken heart.

parts.homeLangDetails: The تَ- prefix marks Pattern V (تَفَعَّلَ), which is the reflexive counterpart to Pattern II. Where Pattern II (دَرَّسَ) means 'to teach,' Pattern V (تَدَرَّسَ) turns the action back on the subject — 'to teach oneself,' 'to study intensively,' or 'to get oneself educated.'

The Pattern II (intensified) form of the root د-ر-س. Pattern II doubles the middle radical to create a causative or intensive meaning — so where دَرَسَ means 'to study,' دَرَّسَ means 'to cause to study,' i.e., 'to teach.' This stem is the base onto which تَ- attaches.

words.translationLong: to study, to get educated, to teach oneself
words.exampleTranslation: The student studied philosophy on his own.

parts.homeLangDetails: In the verbal noun (مصدر) of Pattern II verbs, تَ- is a standard prefix that marks the noun as derived from a Pattern II verb. Here it pairs with the root د-ر-س to form the abstract noun 'teaching' from the verb دَرَّسَ.
From the root د-ر-س (studying, lessons). In the تَفعيل pattern — the standard verbal noun of Pattern II — the root takes this voweled shape to express the abstract activity of teaching.

words.exampleTranslation: The professor loves the teaching profession.

parts.homeLangDetails: The مُ- prefix here marks the active participle of a Pattern II verb (مُفَعِّل pattern). It turns the verb دَرَّسَ (to teach) into 'one who teaches' — a teacher.
From the root د-ر-س (studying, lessons), in its Pattern II form. The active participle voweling (مُدَرِّس) identifies someone whose role is teaching.

words.exampleTranslation: The teachers at this school are exceptional.
