parts.homeLangDetails: The basic (Pattern I) verb from the root ع-ل-م (knowledge, learning). In the فَعِلَ vowel pattern, it means 'to know' or 'to come to know.' This is the foundational form from which علوم, معلوم, عالم, and many other words all branch.

words.translationLong: to know, to come to know, to find out
words.exampleTranslation: The student finally learned the truth.

parts.homeLangDetails: The مَ- (here مَف+عول pattern) marks the passive participle of Pattern I — 'that which is known.' It is one of Arabic's most productive patterns, turning a verbal root into 'the thing that receives the action.'
From the root ع-ل-م (knowledge, learning), in the مَفعول passive participle vowel pattern. The long ū vowel is characteristic of this pattern. Compare: مَكتوب (written), مَفهوم (understood).

words.translationLong: known, established, given (as in a fact)
words.exampleTranslation: It is well known that the Earth is spherical.

parts.homeLangDetails: The broken plural (جمع تكسير) of عِلم (knowledge, science). Arabic often forms plurals by restructuring the internal vowels of a word rather than adding a suffix — عِلم becomes عُلوم in the same way كِتاب becomes كُتُب. Each عِلم is a domain of knowledge; عُلوم is all of them together.

words.exampleTranslation: My sister studies sciences at the university.

parts.homeLang: to employ, to put to work
parts.homeLangDetails: The Pattern II (فَعَّلَ) form of the root ع-م-ل (work, doing). Pattern II doubles the middle radical to make a verb causative or intensive — so where عَمِلَ means 'to work,' عَمَّلَ means 'to make someone work' or 'to employ.' It is a direct causative form.

words.translationShort: to employ, to put to work
words.translationLong: to employ, to hire, to put to work
words.exampleSentence: عَمَّلَ صاحبُ المصنعِ عشرينَ شخصًا جديدًا.
words.exampleTranslation: The factory owner employed twenty new people.

parts.homeLangDetails: In the verbal noun (مصدر) of Pattern II verbs, تَ- is a standard prefix. Here it forms the abstract noun 'employment' or 'activation' from the Pattern II verb عَمَّلَ, following the تَفعيل pattern.

From the root ع-م-ل (work, doing). In the تَفعيل verbal noun pattern of Pattern II, the root takes this characteristic long-ī voweling. The resulting noun names the act of employing or putting something to work.

words.exampleTranslation: The program aims at employing young people.

parts.homeLangDetails: The broken plural of عامِل (worker), from the root ع-م-ل (work, doing). عُمّال is the collective noun for the laboring class — 'the workers.' It serves as the base for the ideological adjective/noun that follows.

This suffix creates abstract nouns and adjectives of belonging, much like English "-ity" or "-ness." عَرَبِيَّة (arabiyya) means "Arabic/Arabness," حُرِّيَّة (hurriyya) means "freedom," and وَطَنِيَّة (wataniyya) means "nationalism/patriotism." You'll also see it in country-related adjectives like مِصْرِيَّة (Egyptian, fem.).

words.translationLong: laborism, the labor movement, working-class ideology
words.exampleTranslation: The party defended the principles of the labor movement.

parts.homeLangDetails: The Pattern III (فاعَلَ) form of the root ع-ل-م (knowledge, learning). Pattern III adds a long ā between the first and second radicals and typically expresses a reciprocal or mutually-directed action between two parties. عالَمَ suggests engaging with, competing against, or interacting with another as an equal — 'to deal with the world' or 'to contend with.'

words.translationShort: to deal with, to contend with
words.translationLong: to interact with, to deal with, to engage with the world
words.exampleTranslation: The merchant dealt with his competitors cleverly.

parts.homeLangDetails: From the root ع-ل-م (knowledge, learning). The فاعَل pattern here functions as a noun of place or scope — 'the realm of what is known' or 'all that exists and can be known.' It is one of Arabic's most beautiful morphological leaps: from 'knowledge' to 'the world' — the world as the totality of what can be learned.

words.exampleTranslation: Children wonder about the secrets of the world.

parts.homeLangDetails: This suffix creates abstract nouns and adjectives of belonging, much like English "-ity" or "-ness." عَرَبِيَّة (arabiyya) means "Arabic/Arabness," حُرِّيَّة (hurriyya) means "freedom," and وَطَنِيَّة (wataniyya) means "nationalism/patriotism." You'll also see it in country-related adjectives like مِصْرِيَّة (Egyptian, fem.).

words.exampleTranslation: The company strives to achieve a global reach.

parts.homeLangDetails: The مَ- prefix here is part of the مَفعَل/مَفعِل pattern, which derives a noun of place from a root — 'the place where the root-action happens.' From ج-ل-س (sitting, sessions), مَجلِس is 'the place of sitting,' i.e., a council chamber or sitting assembly.

From the root ج-ل-س (sitting, sessions). In مَجالِس, this portion represents the root in its broken plural vocalization — مَجلِس (singular, 'council') becomes مَجالِس (plural) by restructuring the internal vowels in the مَفاعِل pattern, one of Arabic's most common broken plural templates.

words.translationLong: councils, assemblies, sitting rooms, legislative chambers
words.exampleTranslation: The municipal councils met to discuss the budget.

parts.homeLangDetails: From the root ج-ل-س (sitting, sessions). This is the verbal noun (masdar) meaning 'sitting' or 'a session', formed on the pattern فُعول.

This suffix creates abstract nouns and adjectives of belonging, much like English "-ity" or "-ness." عَرَبِيَّة (arabiyya) means "Arabic/Arabness," حُرِّيَّة (hurriyya) means "freedom," and وَطَنِيَّة (wataniyya) means "nationalism/patriotism." You'll also see it in country-related adjectives like مِصْرِيَّة (Egyptian, fem.).

words.translationLong: relating to sitting or sessions; sessional
words.exampleTranslation: The meeting was held in a formal sessional manner.

parts.homeLangDetails: From the root ج-ل-س (sitting, sessions). جالِس is the active participle, meaning 'one who is sitting'.

words.exampleSentence: الجالِسون في القاعة ينتظرون بدء المحاضرة.
words.exampleTranslation: Those sitting in the hall are waiting for the lecture to begin.

parts.homeLangDetails: This suffix creates abstract nouns and adjectives of belonging, much like English "-ity" or "-ness." عَرَبِيَّة (arabiyya) means "Arabic/Arabness," حُرِّيَّة (hurriyya) means "freedom," and وَطَنِيَّة (wataniyya) means "nationalism/patriotism." You'll also see it in country-related adjectives like مِصْرِيَّة (Egyptian, fem.).

words.translationLong: written, relating to writing or books; literary
words.exampleSentence: يُفضّل المعلم الامتحانات الكِتابِيَّة على الشفهية.
words.exampleTranslation: The teacher prefers written exams over oral ones.

parts.homeLangDetails: The prefix مَ- signals a place or instrument of the root's action. Here, from the root ك-ت-ب (writing), it gives مَكتَب, a place of writing — an office or desk. مَكاتِب is its broken plural.

This is the plural pattern كَاتِب derived from the root ك-ت-ب. In the broken plural مَكاتِب, the inner vowel pattern shifts to produce the plural of مَكتَب (office/desk). The surface form كاتِب here is part of the broken plural template, not the active participle كاتِب (writer) standing alone.

words.exampleTranslation: The company needs larger offices.

parts.homeLangDetails: From the root ك-ت-ب (writing). كُتّاب is the broken plural of كاتِب (writer, scribe), following the فُعّال pattern used for agent plurals. It also historically referred to a traditional Quranic school where children learned to read and write.

words.exampleSentence: كان الكُتّاب يسجّلون الوثائق بخط اليد.
words.exampleTranslation: The scribes used to record documents by hand.

parts.homeLangDetails: The prefix مَ- marks a place of the root's action. From the root د-ر-س (studying, lessons), it forms مَدرَسة (school — a place of study). مَدارِس is its broken plural.

This reflects the inner vowel template of the broken plural pattern مَفَاعِل, applied to مَدرَسة. The surface دارِس here is the plural template slot, not the active participle دارِس (studier) standing alone.

words.exampleSentence: تم بناء عدة مَدارِس جديدة في المدينة.
words.exampleTranslation: Several new schools were built in the city.

parts.homeLangDetails: From the root د-ر-س (studying, lessons). دِراسة means 'study' or 'studies' as a verbal noun; the -ة drops before the nisba suffix -ِيَّة is added.

This suffix creates abstract nouns and adjectives of belonging, much like English "-ity" or "-ness." عَرَبِيَّة (arabiyya) means "Arabic/Arabness," حُرِّيَّة (hurriyya) means "freedom," and وَطَنِيَّة (wataniyya) means "nationalism/patriotism." You'll also see it in country-related adjectives like مِصْرِيَّة (Egyptian, fem.).

words.exampleTranslation: The academic year began in September.

parts.homeLangDetails: From the root د-ر-س (studying, lessons). دارِس is the active participle meaning 'one who studies' or 'a student/scholar'.

words.exampleSentence: الدارِسون في هذا المعهد متخصصون في اللغويات.
words.exampleTranslation: The students at this institute specialize in linguistics.

parts.homeLangDetails: The prefix مَ- marks a place of the root's action. From the root ع-م-ل (work, doing), it forms مَعمَل (laboratory or workshop — a place of work). مَعامِل is its broken plural.

This reflects the inner vowel template of the broken plural pattern مَفَاعِل, applied to مَعمَل. The surface عامِل here is the plural template slot, not the active participle عامِل (worker) standing alone.

words.exampleTranslation: The university has modern chemistry laboratories.

parts.homeLangDetails: From the root ع-م-ل (work, doing). عامِل is the active participle meaning 'one who works' or 'a worker'.

words.exampleSentence: العامِلون في المصنع يبدأون دوامهم في الصباح الباكر.
words.exampleTranslation: The workers at the factory start their shift early in the morning.

parts.homeLangDetails: From the root ع-م-ل (work, doing). عَمَل is the primary verbal noun meaning 'work', 'action', or 'operation'.

This suffix creates abstract nouns and adjectives of belonging, much like English "-ity" or "-ness." عَرَبِيَّة (arabiyya) means "Arabic/Arabness," حُرِّيَّة (hurriyya) means "freedom," and وَطَنِيَّة (wataniyya) means "nationalism/patriotism." You'll also see it in country-related adjectives like مِصْرِيَّة (Egyptian, fem.).

words.exampleTranslation: The doctors performed a successful surgical operation.

parts.homeLangDetails: The prefix مَ- marks a place of the root's action. From the root خ-ر-ج (exiting, going out), it forms مَخرَج (exit, outlet — a place of going out). مَخارِج is its broken plural.

This reflects the inner vowel template of the broken plural pattern مَفَاعِل, applied to مَخرَج. The surface خارِج here is the plural template slot; it also happens to be the active participle خارِج (one who exits), but in this word it functions as part of the broken plural pattern.

words.translationLong: exits; outlets; ways out; articulation points (in phonetics)
words.exampleSentence: يجب أن تكون المَخارِج واضحة في حالات الطوارئ.
words.exampleTranslation: Exits must be clearly marked in emergencies.

parts.homeLangDetails: From the root خ-ر-ج (exiting, going out). خارِج is the active participle meaning 'going out' or simply 'outside/exterior', used here as a base for the adjective/noun.

This suffix creates abstract nouns and adjectives of belonging, much like English "-ity" or "-ness." عَرَبِيَّة (arabiyya) means "Arabic/Arabness," حُرِّيَّة (hurriyya) means "freedom," and وَطَنِيَّة (wataniyya) means "nationalism/patriotism." You'll also see it in country-related adjectives like مِصْرِيَّة (Egyptian, fem.).

words.translationLong: foreign; external; exterior; ministry of foreign affairs (as noun)
words.exampleSentence: تعمل في وزارة الخارِجِيَّة منذ عشر سنوات.
words.exampleTranslation: She has been working at the Ministry of Foreign Affairs for ten years.

parts.homeLangDetails: From the root خ-ر-ج (exiting, going out). خارِج is the active participle meaning 'one who is going out' or 'one who exits'.

words.translationLong: those who are leaving; those going out
words.exampleSentence: الخارِجون من القاعة يُرجى منهم إغلاق الباب.
words.exampleTranslation: Those leaving the hall are asked to close the door.

parts.homeLangDetails: The prefix مَ- marks a place of the root's action. From the root د-خ-ل (entering, going in), it forms مَدخَل (entrance, entry point — a place of entering). مَداخِل is its broken plural.

This reflects the inner vowel template of the broken plural pattern مَفَاعِل, applied to مَدخَل. The surface داخِل here is the plural template slot; it also resembles the active participle داخِل (one who enters) and the preposition داخِل (inside), but in this word it functions as part of the broken plural pattern.

words.exampleTranslation: The entrances of the building were decorated with flowers.

parts.homeLangDetails: From the root د-خ-ل (entering, going in). داخِل is the active participle — literally 'one who enters' — but it has become a preposition/adjective meaning 'inside' or 'inner.'

This suffix creates abstract nouns and adjectives of belonging, much like English "-ity" or "-ness." عَرَبِيَّة (arabiyya) means "Arabic/Arabness," حُرِّيَّة (hurriyya) means "freedom," and وَطَنِيَّة (wataniyya) means "nationalism/patriotism." You'll also see it in country-related adjectives like مِصْرِيَّة (Egyptian, fem.).

words.translationLong: interiority, the quality of being internal; also used for 'interior ministry' (وزارة الداخلية)
words.exampleSentence: تهتم وزارة الداخلية بشؤون الأمن الداخلي.
words.exampleTranslation: The Ministry of Interior deals with internal security affairs.

parts.homeLangDetails: The active participle of the root د-خ-ل (to enter). داخِل means 'one who enters' or 'entrant.' This is its literal, verbal sense — as opposed to the frozen prepositional use in words like داخِلِيَّة.

words.exampleSentence: الداخلون إلى القاعة يجب أن يُبرزوا بطاقاتهم.
words.exampleTranslation: Those entering the hall must show their cards.

parts.homeLangDetails: A verbal noun (maṣdar) from the root و-ص-ل (to arrive, to connect, to reach). وُصول means 'arrival' or 'access' — the act or fact of reaching something.

This suffix creates abstract nouns and adjectives of belonging, much like English "-ity" or "-ness." عَرَبِيَّة (arabiyya) means "Arabic/Arabness," حُرِّيَّة (hurriyya) means "freedom," and وَطَنِيَّة (wataniyya) means "nationalism/patriotism." You'll also see it in country-related adjectives like مِصْرِيَّة (Egyptian, fem.).

words.translationLong: opportunism (social climbing); also 'accessibility' in modern usage
words.exampleSentence: انتقد الناس وصوليته الواضحة في السياسة.
words.exampleTranslation: People criticized his obvious opportunism in politics.

parts.homeLangDetails: This prefix turns a root into a place or tool word, and it's extremely productive. مَكْتَب (maktab) is "office" (place of writing), مَدْرَسَة (madrasa) is "school" (place of studying), مَطْبَخ (matbakh) is "kitchen" (place of cooking). Spot a م at the start and think "place where this root-action happens."

From the root و-ص-ل (to connect, to reach). واصِل is the active participle — 'one that connects.' The مَ- prefix turns it into a noun of place or instrument, yielding مَواصِل, the plural form referring to means of transport or communication links.

words.translationLong: means of transportation; communication links; connections
words.exampleSentence: المواصل العامة في هذه المدينة ممتازة.
words.exampleTranslation: The public transportation in this city is excellent.

parts.homeLangDetails: Active participle from the root و-ص-ل (to arrive, to connect, to reach). واصِل describes someone who is arriving or has arrived — 'one who connects/reaches.'

words.translationLong: those who are arriving, those who have connected
words.exampleSentence: الواصلون من السفر يحتاجون إلى الراحة.
words.exampleTranslation: Those arriving from travel need rest.

parts.homeLangDetails: This prefix turns a root into a place or tool word, and it's extremely productive. مَكْتَب (maktab) is "office" (place of writing), مَدْرَسَة (madrasa) is "school" (place of studying), مَطْبَخ (matbakh) is "kitchen" (place of cooking). Spot a م at the start and think "place where this root-action happens."

From the root ف-ت-ح (to open, to conquer). مَفاتيح is the broken plural of مِفتاح (key) — the مَ- prefix marks it as an instrument of opening, and فاتيح carries the root meaning. The plural form uses the Arabic broken-plural pattern rather than a suffix.

words.exampleTranslation: I forgot the house keys on the table.

parts.homeLangDetails: Active participle from the root ف-ت-ح (to open, to conquer). فاتِح means 'one who opens' or 'conqueror.' The same root gives مِفتاح (key) and الفتح (the conquest/opening).

words.translationLong: conquerors; those who open or inaugurate
words.exampleSentence: يُعدّ الفاتحون جزءاً مهماً من التاريخ الإسلامي.
words.exampleTranslation: The conquerors are considered an important part of Islamic history.

parts.homeLangDetails: The passive participle of the root ف-ت-ح (to open). مَفتوح means 'that which has been opened' — the thing acted upon, as opposed to فاتِح (the one doing the opening).

This suffix creates abstract nouns and adjectives of belonging, much like English "-ity" or "-ness." عَرَبِيَّة (arabiyya) means "Arabic/Arabness," حُرِّيَّة (hurriyya) means "freedom," and وَطَنِيَّة (wataniyya) means "nationalism/patriotism." You'll also see it in country-related adjectives like مِصْرِيَّة (Egyptian, fem.).

words.translationLong: openness; receptivity; the quality of being open
words.exampleSentence: تتميز هذه الجامعة بمفتوحية أكاديمية عالية.
words.exampleTranslation: This university is distinguished by a high degree of academic openness.

parts.homeLangDetails: Active participle from the root ط-ل-ب (to seek, to request, to demand). طالِب literally means 'one who seeks' — and by extension 'student' (one who seeks knowledge). The Taliban takes its name from this same word.

words.translationLong: students; those who seek or request
words.exampleSentence: الطالبون في هذا الفصل متحمسون جداً.
words.exampleTranslation: The students in this class are very enthusiastic.

parts.homeLangDetails: This prefix turns a root into a place or tool word, and it's extremely productive. مَكْتَب (maktab) is "office" (place of writing), مَدْرَسَة (madrasa) is "school" (place of studying), مَطْبَخ (matbakh) is "kitchen" (place of cooking). Spot a م at the start and think "place where this root-action happens."

From the root ط-ل-ب (to seek, to demand). مَطالِب is the broken plural of مَطلَب (demand, requirement) — the مَ- prefix marks it as the object or focus of seeking. The plural captures 'things that are sought' or 'demands.'

words.exampleTranslation: The workers submitted their demands to the management.

parts.homeLangDetails: A verbal noun (maṣdar) from the root ط-ل-ب (to seek, to request). طَلَب is the act of requesting — more neutral and procedural than مَطلَب (a pressing demand or requirement).

words.translationLong: requests; applications; orders (as in a food/product order)
words.exampleSentence: يمكنك تقديم طلباتك عبر الموقع الإلكتروني.
words.exampleTranslation: You can submit your requests through the website.

parts.homeLangDetails: Active participle from the root ق-ر-أ (to read, to recite). قارِئ means 'one who reads' or 'a reader.' The same root gives القرآن (the Recitation) and قِراءة (a reading).

words.translationLong: readers; those who read or recite
words.exampleTranslation: Regular readers develop more quickly.

parts.homeLangDetails: Here the مَ- prefix functions as a marker of the passive participle pattern (مَفعول), meaning 'that which is read.' It is not indicating a place or instrument in this case, but the thing acted upon by the root.

From the root ق-ر-أ (to read, to recite). مَقروء (more standardly spelled) is the passive participle — 'that which has been read.' The form مَقاروء here is an augmented/plural-like form sometimes used to refer to reading material or texts.

words.translationLong: that which is read; reading material; assigned texts
words.exampleSentence: المقاروء المقررة هذا الفصل متنوعة وشيقة.
words.exampleTranslation: The assigned reading material this semester is varied and interesting.
words.flag: Non-standard form — مَقروء is the standard passive participle; مَقاروء may be a pedagogical or augmented variant. Verify usage.

parts.homeLangDetails: A verbal noun (maṣdar) pattern from the root ق-ر-أ (to read, to recite). قِراءة means 'a reading' or 'a recitation.' The same root gives القرآن ('the Recitation') and قارِئ ('reader').

words.translationLong: readings; recitations; interpretations; variant readings (as in Quranic traditions)
words.exampleSentence: هناك قراءات متعددة لهذا النص الأدبي.
words.exampleTranslation: There are multiple readings of this literary text.

parts.homeLangDetails: From the root س-ف-ر (to travel, to journey). سافَرَ is the past tense third-person masculine singular — 'he traveled.' The root also gives سَفَر (travel), سَفير (ambassador), and سِفر (a book/scroll in religious contexts).
The suffix -ت here marks the verb as third-person feminine singular in the past tense — 'she traveled.' This same -ت appears on all feminine past-tense verbs in Arabic.

words.exampleSentence: سافرت أختي إلى فرنسا الشهر الماضي.
words.exampleTranslation: My sister traveled to France last month.

parts.homeLangDetails: The مُ- prefix marks an active participle in Arabic verb patterns. Here it turns the root س-ف-ر into 'one who travels' — a traveler. Think of it like English '-er' on a verb stem.

From the root س-ف-ر (s-f-r), which carries the core idea of traveling, journeying, or departing. The same root gives you سَفَر (a journey) and سِفَارة (embassy — originally a place of diplomatic travel).

words.exampleTranslation: The travelers are waiting at the airport.

parts.homeLangDetails: From the root س-ف-ر (s-f-r), the core idea of traveling or going on a trip. سَفَر is the noun form meaning 'a journey' or 'travel' in the abstract.

This suffix creates abstract nouns and adjectives of belonging, much like English "-ity" or "-ness." عَرَبِيَّة (arabiyya) means "Arabic/Arabness," حُرِّيَّة (hurriyya) means "freedom," and وَطَنِيَّة (wataniyya) means "nationalism/patriotism." You'll also see it in country-related adjectives like مِصْرِيَّة (Egyptian, fem.).

words.translationLong: travel (adjective), relating to a journey; travel bag/trip (in context)
words.exampleTranslation: I bought a new travel bag.

parts.homeLangDetails: The active participle of the root ح-م-ل (h-m-l), meaning 'to carry' or 'to bear.' حامِل is 'one who carries' — a carrier or bearer. The same root gives حِمل (a load) and مَحمول (carried/mobile).

words.exampleSentence: حامِلون الأعلام مَشَوا في مُقَدِّمة الاحتِفال.
words.exampleTranslation: The flag bearers walked at the front of the celebration.

parts.homeLangDetails: The مَ- prefix here marks a passive participle — 'that which is carried.' It's one of the most productive prefixes in Arabic, turning verb roots into nouns of result or object. Compare مَكتوب (written/letter) from ك-ت-ب.

From the root ح-م-ل (h-m-l), meaning to carry or bear a load. The surface form here is حمول rather than حمل because the passive participle pattern (maf'ūl: مَفعول) inserts a long vowel, giving مَحمول (carried, mobile).

words.translationLong: cargoes, payloads, mobile devices (plural of مَحمول)
words.exampleTranslation: The cargoes were inspected at customs.

parts.homeLangDetails: From the root ح-م-ل (h-m-l), to carry or bear. حِمال is a noun pattern (fi'āl) associated with the instrument or manner of an action — something used for carrying or the act of carrying repeatedly.

This letter, taa' marbuuta, is the most reliable femininity signal in Arabic. It sits at the end of words and is usually pronounced as a soft "a" or "at." مُدَرِّس (mudarris) is "male teacher," مُدَرِّسَة (mudarrisa) is "female teacher." It also forms abstract nouns like حُرِّيَّة (hurriyya, "freedom") and مَدِينَة (madiina, "city").

words.exampleTranslation: The bag's strap broke suddenly.

parts.homeLangDetails: The canonical active participle pattern (fā'il: فاعِل) built on the root ف-ع-ل (f-'-l), which is the quintessential Arabic root for 'doing' or 'acting.' فاعِل is so central that it literally names the grammatical subject in Arabic grammar — 'the one who does.'

words.exampleTranslation: The active participants in society change the world.

parts.homeLangDetails: The مَ- prefix here forms the مَفعول (maf'ūl) pattern — the passive participle, meaning 'that which is acted upon.' مَفعول is itself the Arabic grammatical term for the direct object, making this word delightfully self-referential.

The plural of مَفعول follows the broken plural pattern مَفاعيل, built on the root ف-ع-ل (f-'-l). Broken plurals in Arabic restructure the word internally rather than adding a suffix — the long ي and the rearranged vowels are the plural signal, not a separate morpheme.

words.translationLong: direct objects (grammar), effects, results acted upon
words.exampleSentence: المَفاعيل في الجُملة تأتي بعد الفِعل.
words.exampleTranslation: The direct objects in the sentence come after the verb.
words.flag: broken plural: مَفاعيل is the broken plural of مَفعول; the internal vowel change encodes plurality rather than a separable suffix morpheme

parts.homeLangDetails: From the root ف-ع-ل (f-'-l), the core Arabic root for doing and acting. فِعل is the noun meaning 'action' or 'verb' — it is literally the word Arabic grammarians use for 'verb,' making it the heart of the language's grammatical vocabulary.

This suffix creates abstract nouns and adjectives of belonging, much like English "-ity" or "-ness." عَرَبِيَّة (arabiyya) means "Arabic/Arabness," حُرِّيَّة (hurriyya) means "freedom," and وَطَنِيَّة (wataniyya) means "nationalism/patriotism." You'll also see it in country-related adjectives like مِصْرِيَّة (Egyptian, fem.).

words.translationLong: verbal (grammar), practical, action-based, relating to deeds
words.exampleTranslation: The verbal sentence begins with a verb.
